name: leek pepper lentil skillet
instructions:
Sauté leeks, garlic, and thyme in olive oil until tender — leeks should soften and pick up flavor, not collapse like for a soffrito. Remove from heat and let the sauté cool.

In a bowl, combine cold cooked lentils with raw cucumbers OR raw bell peppers (orange recommended). DO NOT add the lentils to the pan — they stay cold and raw alongside the vegetables.

Toss the lentil-and-vegetable mixture with red wine vinegar. If using bell peppers, add a tiny bit of dijon mustard. If using cucumbers, no dijon — cucumber stays clean.

Fold the cooled leek-garlic-thyme sauté into the bowl. Season with salt and black pepper to taste.
notes:
Mediterranean lentil salad. Built around a cold lentil + raw vegetable base, finished with a sautéed-then-cooled leek-garlic-thyme aromatic and red wine vinegar.

LENTILS STAY COLD AND RAW (NOT IN THE PAN): the lentils must be cooked, but they don't enter the sauté pan. This is the same rule as french-lentil-salad — cold lentils play with raw vegetables and an acidic vinaigrette. If you cook the lentils in the pan, they start demanding tomatoes, mushrooms, and stock, and the dish veers into italian lentil stew territory (which is a different recipe entirely). Keep them cold.

LEEKS + GARLIC + THYME ARE ALL NON-OPTIONAL: this is the aromatic backbone. The leek-thyme combination is the canonical mediterranean-skillet use of thyme (covered in the herbs and greens article). Garlic rides alongside; don't skip it.

RED WINE VINEGAR IS CORE: red wine vinegar is part of the dressing, not optional. It works specifically because the vegetables are raw — the vinegar's sharpness cuts the raw cucumber or bell pepper and pulls everything together. (In the french-lentil-salad version, vinegar is one of several flexible acid choices alongside a cream base; here it's the canonical acid, full stop.)

FRESH VEGETABLE FORK: cucumbers OR bell peppers, not both.
- CUCUMBERS: lean and clean. The cucumber stays crisp; no dijon needed.
- BELL PEPPERS (orange recommended): sweeter and more substantial. With bell peppers, a small amount of dijon mustard adds the bright sharpness the bell pepper alone doesn't quite deliver.

NO STOCK, NO TOMATOES, NO OTHER HERBS, NO CREAM: stock pushes toward a chicken-and-vegetable stew; tomatoes push italian-stew; other herbs muddy the leek-thyme axis; cream pushes french. Holding these out is what keeps the dish in its own distinct mediterranean lentil-salad territory.
